title: How Trauma Creates Sensory Processing Differences
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Trauma rewires the nervous system, leading to sensory processing issues like hypersensitivity and numbness. Learn how to heal and reclaim your senses.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The scene is quiet and still, evoking a sense of gentle restoration and the beauty of a healed history. The focus is on the golden seams, suggesting the nervous system's pathways being carefully mended.
finalH2: The Body as Compass: Navigating a New Sensory World
htmlContent:
The body that carries you through your day is not the same one that endured the unthinkable. It has been fundamentally rewired, its operating system overwritten by a threat-detection program that never truly powers down, leaving you to navigate a world of sensory data that feels both overwhelming and muted at the very same time.

The Ghost in the Machine: When Sensory Gating Fails

For most people, the brain performs a constant, unconscious triage of incoming sensory information ~ the hum of a refrigerator, the texture of a shirt, the distant siren... filtering out the irrelevant so that only important signals reach conscious awareness. This process, known as sensory gating, allows for focus and presence. But for a nervous system shaped by trauma, this gating mechanism is often one of the first casualties. The sentinels at the gate become hypervigilant and indiscriminate, treating every piece of sensory data as a potential threat. It’s not that your hearing is more acute; it’s that the system for deciding what to do with sounds has been compromised, leaving raw, unprocessed data to flood the circuits.

This resistance to certain sounds, textures, or lights is not a personal failing; it is a physiological echo of a time when survival depended on detecting the smallest environmental shift. The work is not to force the system into compliance, but to begin the slow process of teaching it that the war it is still fighting has ended. This happens not through intellect, but through the repeated introduction of genuinely neutral or pleasant sensory experiences, allowing the gating mechanism to cautiously come back online. It turns out to be the beginning of reclaiming the body from the ghost of threats past, of learning to inhabit a world that is not a minefield but a space of sensation.

The Architecture of Numbness and Hypersensitivity

The paradox of a trauma-altered sensory system is its simultaneous state of hypersensitivity and numbness, a duality that can make one feel alien in their own skin. One might detect the subtlest shift in another’s tone of voice ~ a nervous system tuned to social threat... yet be completely disconnected from the feeling of their own feet on the ground. What emerges is not a contradiction; it is the architecture of survival. The sensory apparatus for threat detection is maximized, while interoception ~ the felt sense of the body’s internal state - is muted. It was safer to not feel the terror, rage, or freeze within the body, so the volume was turned down.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

Sound, Light, and Touch: A Re-Tuned World

When the sensory system is rewired by trauma, the world’s fundamental elements are experienced through a different filter. Sounds are often the most noticeably altered. Misophonia or a general hyperacusis can emerge, where everyday noises feel painfully loud. This isn’t about the ears, but about the amygdala and auditory cortex working on high alert, flagging ordinary sounds as danger signals. The brain loses its ability to distinguish between a predator’s rustle and a plastic bag’s; to the traumatized nervous system, they are processed with the same alarm, triggering a cascade of physiological responses from a sound others might not even register.

Seriously.

Similarly, light can become fraught. Bright, artificial lights, especially fluorescents, can be intensely dysregulating. The visual system, like the auditory one, is on high alert, and chaotic stimuli can overwhelm its processing capacity, leading to shutdown. The world of touch also becomes complex. One might crave connection yet find being touched overwhelming. The skin, the body’s largest sensory organ, holds the memory of boundary breaches. The work, as pioneered by researchers like Peter Levine, is not to desensitize the system by force, but to create new, reparative sensory experiences. It involves finding the specific frequency of sound, quality of light, and nature of touch that the nervous system can register as safe, offering small, digestible moments of sensory nourishment that can, over time, recalibrate the entire system.

The Social Nervous System and Sensory Overload

Our sensory experience is not a solo endeavor; it is deeply intertwined with our interactions. The polyvagal theory illuminates how our nervous systems are in constant, subconscious conversation, using cues in facial expression and tone of voice to assess for safety. For a system shaped by relational trauma, this social-sensory channel is a primary source of dysregulation. The very cues meant to signal safety may have been associated with danger, creating a painful internal conflict. The desire for connection pulls us toward others, while the body’s threat-detection system screams for retreat, presenting as a genuine social anxiety that feels more like a cellular aversion than a fear of judgment.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link).

Popular culture frames social ease as a matter of confidence, a skill to be learned. But for the trauma survivor, it is a matter of physiology. The work is not about learning what to say, but about helping the body unlearn its association between connection and threat. It is a slow titration of social engagement, finding the people and contexts that feel genuinely nourishing to the nervous system, and building from there. It is about discovering that safety is not something another can give you, but a state you can cultivate within yourself, even in the presence of others.

From Fragmentation to Integration: Reclaiming the Senses

The fragmentation of sensory experience is a hallmark of complex trauma. The world is either too much or not enough, a cacophony of input or a muted terrain of disconnection. This results from a nervous system that sacrificed integration for survival. The smooth flow of information between the senses, body, and awareness has been disrupted. Reclaiming the senses is therefore a process of integration, of gently rebuilding the neural pathways that allow for a more coherent experience of reality. It is the work of moving from being at the mercy of one’s sensory environment to becoming an active participant in its creation.

By consciously choosing where to place our attention, we begin to shift the balance. We are not ignoring the hypersensitivities or numbness; we are introducing another possibility. What emerges is the essence of moving from a reactive to a creative orientation to life. We start to build an internal locus of safety, a felt sense that we can be a resource for ourselves regardless of the external environment. It is the slow, deliberate work of weaving the fragmented pieces of our sensory world back into a cohesive whole, not by force, but by creating the conditions of safety and presence that allow for their organic integration. This is how we learn to navigate the complexities of relational trauma and find our way back to an embodied life.

The Body as Compass: Navigating a New Sensory World

As the nervous system heals and the sensory world comes back online in a more integrated way, a new capacity emerges: using the body as a compass. For so long, the body’s signals were either too loud (panic) or too quiet (numbness) to be a reliable guide. They were alarms, not informants. But as the background noise of threat subsides, we can discern the more subtle signals the body constantly sends. We notice the quiet “yes” of an expansion in the chest, or the subtle “no” of a tightening in the gut. These are the detailed language of an intelligent system communicating its truth.

For soothing sensory overwhelm around the eyes and forehead, consider TheraPearl Eye Mask (paid link).

This capacity is the antidote to the gaslighting that so often accompanies complex trauma. When you can feel the truth of your experience in your own tissues, it becomes much harder for anyone to argue you out of it. The body becomes the ultimate arbiter of reality. This doesn’t mean we are ruled by impulse; it means we have access to a deeper, more integrated source of information to bring into dialogue with our rational mind. It is the reunion of body and mind, the end of the internal war. The sensory world is no longer a battlefield, but a rich, informative field to be navigated with a compass that is finally, reliably, our own. The question then becomes not how to silence the body, but how to listen more deeply to the wisdom it has been offering all along.

Think about that for a second.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel both numb and overly sensitive at the same time?

This paradox is a core feature of a nervous system shaped by trauma. The parts of your sensory system responsible for detecting external threats become hypersensitive for survival, while the system that registers your own internal bodily states (interoception) becomes numbed or muted to protect you from overwhelming emotions. They are two sides of the same protective strategy, a brilliant adaptation to an unsafe environment that is no longer serving you.

Can sensory processing differences from trauma be healed?

Yes, absolutely. Healing involves gradually and safely re-educating the nervous system. Through practices that build your capacity to be present with your body and by slowly introducing neutral or pleasant sensory input, you can help your system learn that the world is no longer the dangerous place it once was. This process, often undertaken with a trauma-informed therapist, helps recalibrate your sensory gating and expands your window of tolerance for sensory information.

Sometimes the simplest tool for processing is a good notebook like Moleskine Classic Notebook (paid link).

Ultimately, the path is not about returning to a pre-trauma state, as that person no longer exists. It is about integrating the wisdom gained through the experience, however brutal, and forging a new relationship with a world of sensation that is no longer defined exclusively by threat. It is about discovering that the same nervous system that can register striking pain also holds the capacity for striking connection and pleasure. What would become possible if you began to relate to your sensory sensitivities not as a pathology to be fixed, but as the dial on a highly sophisticated instrument that you are finally learning how to read?
